title: The New Jobs AI Is Creating That Didn't Exist 3 Years Ago
description: From prompt engineers to AI safety researchers, AI is creating entirely new career paths with six-figure salaries. Explore the emerging roles and how to break into them.
content:
Every Technological Revolution Creates More Jobs Than It Destroys

The internet killed travel agencies, video rental stores, and classified ad departments. It also created web developers, social media managers, UX designers, SEO specialists, cloud architects, and about ten million other roles that nobody saw coming.

AI is following the exact same pattern. And if you're paying attention, you can see the new careers forming in real time.

As Sam Altman, CEO of OpenAI, has noted: "AI will eliminate some categories of jobs, but it's already creating entirely new ones at an incredible pace. The new jobs are often better paid, more creative, and more interesting than the ones they replace."

He's right. Let's look at the jobs that barely existed — or didn't exist at all — three years ago, and are now pulling six-figure salaries.

1. Prompt Engineer

Salary range: $90,000 - $250,000

This is probably the most talked-about new AI role, and for good reason. Prompt engineers craft the inputs that make large language models produce useful, accurate, and context-appropriate outputs. Think of them as translators between human intent and machine capability.

Three years ago, this job title appeared on virtually zero job boards. Today, a search on Indeed returns thousands of listings. Glassdoor reports median salaries well above $130K, with senior roles at major tech companies exceeding $200K.

The best part? You don't need a traditional tech background. Many successful prompt engineers come from writing, journalism, teaching, and linguistics. If you understand language and logic, you have a head start.

2. AI Safety Researcher

Salary range: $140,000 - $350,000+

As AI systems become more powerful, ensuring they behave safely and predictably has become one of the most important — and well-funded — problems in technology. AI safety researchers study how to align AI behavior with human values, prevent harmful outputs, and build systems that fail gracefully.

Companies like Anthropic, Google DeepMind, and OpenAI have entire teams dedicated to safety research. Governments are funding safety institutes. And the salaries reflect the urgency — senior AI safety researchers are among the highest-paid people in tech.

This field draws from computer science, mathematics, philosophy, cognitive science, and even political science. It's one of the rare careers where a philosophy PhD might actually out-earn a software engineer.

3. Machine Learning Operations (MLOps) Engineer

Salary range: $120,000 - $220,000

Building an AI model is one thing. Deploying it reliably in production, monitoring its performance, managing data pipelines, and ensuring it doesn't degrade over time? That's MLOps.

Think of MLOps engineers as the bridge between the data scientists who create AI models and the real-world systems that run them. It's a deeply practical, engineering-focused role that has exploded in demand as companies move from AI experiments to AI deployment.

LinkedIn's Jobs on the Rise report has listed MLOps as one of the fastest-growing titles for three consecutive years. If you have a background in DevOps, cloud engineering, or software development, the transition to MLOps is highly natural.

4. AI Content Strategist

Salary range: $85,000 - $160,000

Here's a role that proves AI isn't just creating technical jobs. AI content strategists figure out how organizations should use generative AI for marketing, communications, and brand storytelling — while maintaining quality, authenticity, and legal compliance.

They develop AI content policies, design workflows that blend human creativity with AI efficiency, and train teams on how to use tools like ChatGPT, Midjourney, and video generation platforms without producing garbage or getting sued.

If you have a marketing, communications, or content background, this role is essentially your existing career plus AI fluency. And companies are paying a premium for people who have both.

5. AI Ethics Consultant

Salary range: $110,000 - $200,000

Not to be confused with the AI ethics officers working inside companies, AI ethics consultants are independent advisors who help organizations navigate the ethical, legal, and reputational risks of AI deployment.

They audit algorithms for bias, review AI strategies for regulatory compliance, and advise leadership on responsible AI practices. With the EU AI Act now in effect and similar regulations rolling out globally, demand for this expertise has surged.

Backgrounds in law, policy, social science, and compliance are natural fits. Several consulting firms — including Deloitte, McKinsey, and specialized boutiques — have stood up entire AI ethics practices in the last two years.

6. Synthetic Media Specialist

Salary range: $95,000 - $180,000

AI-generated images, video, audio, and 3D environments are transforming entertainment, advertising, education, and corporate training. Synthetic media specialists create, manage, and quality-control this AI-generated content.

Think about it: somebody needs to produce those AI-generated product shots, virtual training environments, personalized video campaigns, and synthetic voice experiences. And somebody needs to ensure they're high quality, brand-consistent, and not accidentally offensive.

Real job postings from companies like Netflix, Epic Games, and agencies like WPP now specifically list "synthetic media" or "generative media" experience as requirements. Indeed shows these roles have grown from near-zero to thousands of postings in under three years.

7. AI Whisperer (Human-AI Interaction Designer)

Salary range: $100,000 - $175,000

This role goes by several names, but the core function is the same: designing how humans and AI systems interact. Not just chatbot interfaces — but entire experiences where AI is embedded into products, services, and workflows.

How should a doctor interact with an AI diagnostic tool? How should a customer experience an AI-powered financial advisor? How should an employee collaborate with an AI assistant without losing their own skills? These are design questions that require deep understanding of both human psychology and AI capabilities.

It sits at the intersection of UX design, cognitive science, and AI product management. And it's one of those roles that gets more important — and more complex — as AI gets more capable.

What All These Roles Have in Common

Look across all seven of these emerging careers and you'll notice a pattern: they all sit at the intersection of AI capability and human judgment. None of them are purely technical. All of them require understanding people — how they think, what they need, what they value, and how they work.

That's the real signal here. AI isn't just creating jobs for engineers and programmers. It's creating jobs for communicators, strategists, ethicists, designers, and domain experts who can bridge the gap between what AI can do and what humans need it to do.

As Jensen Huang, CEO of NVIDIA, put it: "We're at the beginning of a new computing era. The number of jobs this technology creates will be far greater than anyone currently imagines."

The data from Glassdoor, Indeed, and LinkedIn all tell the same story: these roles are growing fast, paying well, and accessible to people from a wide range of backgrounds. But the window to be an early mover is right now — before these roles become as competitive as traditional tech positions.

How to Position Yourself

Pick the role that aligns closest with your existing skills and interests. Then:

  • Start learning the fundamentals through free courses on Coursera, DeepLearning.AI, and Google
  • Build a portfolio of relevant projects — even personal ones count
  • Connect with people already in these roles on LinkedIn
  • Apply early and often — companies are still figuring out what these roles look like, which means they're open to non-traditional candidates

The careers of the future aren't coming. They're already here, hiring today, and paying more than many people realize.
